case_name: Lithium reduction from brackish groundwater for municipal drinking water supply
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for lithium-contaminated groundwater intended for drinking water supply.

input_data.scenario_background: A small municipal utility relies on a brackish groundwater wellfield and has identified elevated lithium in raw water during expanded monitoring. The utility needs a treatment approach that can reliably lower lithium for drinking water production using established technologies and moderate operator skill. The system prefers options with manageable energy demand and residuals handling, and does not want an unnecessarily complex treatment train unless clearly justified.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should achieve substantial lithium reduction suitable for drinking water supply while maintaining stable finished-water quality and practical municipal operation.
secondary_goals: avoid excessive energy consumption where possible

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: concentrate or waste stream handling is possible but should be minimized; no appetite for highly complex chemical regeneration systems
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: stable year-round operation with conventional municipal staffing

core_unit_functions.cartridge_prefiltration: protect downstream membranes by removing fine particulates
core_unit_functions.reverse_osmosis: reject lithium and other dissolved salts from brackish groundwater
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust permeate chemistry for corrosion control and finished-water stability

applicability_rationale: lithium is a dissolved monovalent ion that is not effectively removed by conventional clarification alone
for groundwater with elevated TDS, reverse osmosis is a well-established drinking-water process that provides practical lithium reduction
the source water is already brackish, so a desalting process can be justified without creating an artificially complex train
pretreatment needs are moderate because turbidity and iron are low
this option is the clearest proven municipal approach under an explicit lithium-removal goal
potential_risks: lithium rejection may be lower than for divalent ions, so pilot testing and recovery optimization are important
membrane scaling control and routine cleaning are necessary
post-treatment stabilization is needed because permeate can be low in alkalinity and hardness

core_unit_functions.cartridge_prefiltration: remove fine suspended matter before membrane treatment
core_unit_functions.nanofiltration: provide partial dissolved-ion reduction with lower pressure than reverse osmosis

applicability_rationale: nanofiltration is a recognized membrane option for reducing dissolved constituents in groundwater
it may offer lower energy demand than reverse osmosis in some applications
the simple groundwater matrix and low turbidity support membrane operation with limited pretreatment
potential_risks: lithium rejection is often weaker for nanofiltration than for reverse osmosis because lithium is small and monovalent
finished water may not consistently achieve the desired lithium reduction without conservative design or blending control

core_unit_functions.cartridge_prefiltration: remove particles that could foul the selective media bed
core_unit_functions.selective_ion_exchange_or_specialty_adso: target lithium through specialized sorption or exchange mechanisms
core_unit_functions.post_filtration: capture fines and polish treated water
core_unit_functions.disinfection: provide final barrier for drinking water supply

applicability_rationale: specialized lithium-selective media technologies are known in industrial and emerging treatment contexts
this route could reduce energy demand relative to high-pressure membranes if proven under site-specific conditions
the train is compact and can be configured for groundwater treatment
potential_risks: drinking-water deployment is less established than reverse osmosis
performance can be sensitive to competing ions such as sodium and hardness
media exhaustion, regeneration chemistry, or replacement logistics may be difficult for a small utility
site-specific pilot data would be essential before selection

core_unit_functions.lime_softening_or_conventional_coagulati: remove hardness or particulates through conventional chemical treatment
core_unit_functions.sedimentation: separate bulk solids formed during treatment

applicability_rationale: this is a familiar municipal treatment framework with conventional operation
it may improve general water quality and reduce hardness if that is a secondary objective
some utilities may initially consider it because it is operationally familiar
potential_risks: conventional clarification and softening are not reliable primary methods for dissolved lithium removal
chemical and sludge production may increase without solving the main contaminant problem
this option risks underperforming relative to the explicit lithium objective

core_unit_functions.electrodialysis_or_electrodialysis_rever: separate dissolved ions using electrically driven ion-selective membranes
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust product water chemistry after desalting

applicability_rationale: electromembrane desalting can remove dissolved ions from brackish groundwater
it is a technically plausible alternative where desalting is needed
potential_risks: less straightforward and less commonly selected than reverse osmosis for small conventional municipal systems
lithium-selective performance may be uncertain without pilot testing
requires skilled control of electrical and membrane systems
still produces a concentrated waste stream

preferred_rank_pattern: reverse_osmosis_based_desalting should rank first for explicit lithium reduction in brackish groundwater drinking water treatment
nanofiltration may appear as a lower-ranked membrane alternative but should not outrank reverse osmosis for lithium
conventional clarification_only approaches should rank below membrane-based options because lithium is dissolved and monovalent

must_reflect: moderate_cost_constraint allows proven membrane treatment but disfavors unnecessarily novel or highly complex specialty systems
conventional_operation preference should favor established municipal desalting trains with simple pretreatment and post-treatment
recommendations should acknowledge concentrate handling and avoid overcomplicating pretreatment for low-turbidity groundwater

must_contain: that lithium is difficult to remove with standard conventional coagulation and filtration
why reverse osmosis is the most established and defensible option for dissolved lithium reduction in brackish groundwater
why nanofiltration and specialty media rank lower due to weaker certainty or maturity for lithium control

should_mention: uncertain rejection of small monovalent ions compared with divalent salts
need for post-treatment stabilization after desalting
importance of pilot testing for lithium-specific removal performance
